1:
Ps 32 superscription: 'A Maskil of David' — maskil is a term of uncertain technical meaning (from sakal, to be wise/successful): possibly 'instructional psalm,' 'contemplative,' or a musical term. Ps 32 is classified as one of the Seven Penitential Psalms in Christian tradition (with Pss 6, 38, 51, 102, 130, 143), making it a primary text for sacramental confession from patristic times forward. Augustine reportedly had this psalm written on his bedroom wall.

Three Hebrew forgiveness terms in vv1-2: (1) nasa pesha (lifted/carried away transgression) — pesha is willful breach of covenant; nasa (to lift, carry) — the bearing-away of guilt, the root of the scapegoat ritual (Lev 16:22). (2) kesuy chata (sin covered) — chasah (to cover) — concealing sin from divine view, used of the atonement lid (kapporet, mercy seat) on the ark. (3) lo-yachshov avon (not reckoning iniquity) — the forensic term: God does not enter the debt on the ledger. Paul cites v2 in Rom 4:7-8 as evidence of imputed righteousness.
2: 'Blessed is the man to whom the LORD does not reckon iniquity, and in whose spirit there is no deceit' — lo-yachshov (does not reckon/count) — the accounting metaphor: chashav (to reckon, calculate, plan) used of commercial ledgers. The forgiven person's account shows no debt. 'In whose spirit there is no deceit' (ein rucho remiyyah) — remiyyah (deceit, slackness) — the forgiven person is also honest: the blessing is not amnesty for continued deception but the relief of genuine confession and clean standing.
3: 'When I kept silent about my sin, my bones wasted away through my constant groaning' — the physical effects of unconfessed sin. ANE medical texts did not separate physical illness from spiritual/moral disorder — the body bore the consequences of covenant violation. 'Bones' (atzamot) as the structural framework of the person: when they waste, the whole person is in collapse. The 'constant groaning' (beshagati kol-hayom) — involuntary sounds of suffering that escape despite the silence about sin.
4: 'For day and night your hand was heavy upon me; my strength was dried up as by the heat of summer' — 'the heavy hand of YHWH' (yad YHWH nichbedah alai) — divine pressure as weight: the physical sense of being crushed. The image appears in Ezek 3:14; 1 Sam 5:6 (the heavy hand on the Philistines with the captured ark). The summer heat (charbonei kayitz) dries up moisture — the unconfessed-sin body has no internal resources left. Selah marks a liturgical pause, probably an instrumental interlude.
5: 'I acknowledged my sin to you and I did not cover my iniquity; I said, I will confess my transgressions to the LORD, and you forgave the iniquity of my sin' — the resolution: acknowledgment ends the hiding, and divine forgiveness follows immediately. 'You forgave' — the Hebrew is literally 'you lifted/carried (nasa) the iniquity of my sin' — the same bearing-away term as v1. The act of confession triggers the same forgiveness that the opening beatitude described.
6: 'Therefore let everyone who is godly offer prayer to you at a time when you may be found' — let chesed (one who is godly, lit. one who practices covenant-love) — the community is invited into the same prayer. 'At a time when you may be found' (leet metzo) — divine accessibility is not guaranteed: there are times when the door of prayer is particularly open. This urgency about the timing of prayer echoes Isa 55:6 ('seek the LORD while he may be found').
7: 'You are a hiding place for me; you preserve me from trouble; you surround me with shouts of deliverance' — seter (hiding place, shelter — the divine protective enclosure). The sanctuary itself was called a place of divine shelter (Ps 27:5; 31:20). 'Shouts of deliverance' (rannei pallet) surrounding the forgiven person — the joyful community-shout of rescue that answers the groaning of vv3-4. The liturgical movement: groaning → confession → prayer → divine shelter and rescue-shout.
8: 'I will instruct you and teach you in the way you should go; I will counsel you with my eye upon you' — the speaker shifts to YHWH (or possibly a priest/wisdom teacher). The wisdom-teacher role mirrors the relationship of Proverbs: the experienced teacher who has walked the path of confession and forgiveness now guides others. 'With my eye upon you' (etsah alecha eini) — attentive guidance, watching the path. This contrasts with the mule/horse of v9 who require bit and bridle because they do not look to their guide.
9: 'Be not like a horse or a mule, without understanding, which must be curbed with bit and bridle, or it will not stay near you' — the wisdom fable of the untamed animal requiring physical restraint. ANE wisdom literature (Proverbs, Amenemope) commonly uses animal analogies: the stubborn ox, the foolish animal that must be beaten into compliance. The human who requires divine 'bit and bridle' (crisis, suffering) rather than responsive wisdom is the fool of Prov 12:1. The confession of v5 is what the responsive person does; the unresponsive person requires the heavy hand of v4.
10: 'Many are the sorrows of the wicked, but steadfast love surrounds the one who trusts in the LORD' — the two circles: makkot harbeh (many strikes/sorrows) surrounding the wicked, and chesed (steadfast love) surrounding the trusting. The one who trusts (ha-boteach) is encircled by covenant love — the same chesed vocabulary that makes David's covenant (2 Sam 7:15) permanent. Batach (trust) is the behavioral posture that positions the person within the circle of chesed.
11: 'Be glad in the LORD, and rejoice, O righteous, and shout for joy, all you upright in heart' — the closing summons to communal praise, matching the opening beatitude (v1). The threefold command (be glad, rejoice, shout) encompasses the whole person: inner gladness, expressed rejoicing, and public shout. The yishreh-lev (upright in heart) of the closing verse echoes the 'no deceit in spirit' of v2: the community of the forgiven is the community of the inwardly honest, and their praise arises from the release of forgiveness.
